# Building an Open-Source Agentic AI Equity Research Tool

[![Richard Goerwitz](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*PcKY3d6N2MsIN_u3_ia9Dw.jpeg)](https://achaar.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--172783ed6961---------------------------------------)

[Richard Goerwitz](https://achaar.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--172783ed6961---------------------------------------)

Follow

12 min read

·

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*_8yNngBs5JQKhWA7DWFa-Q.png)

I spent two months building an agentic AI research tool that screens international stocks. It costs relatively little to run, rejects around 90% of already pre-screened candidate equities, and allows me to focus more energy on the ones I really care about.

This article details how the agentic AI system works, what it does well, where it struggles, and what you should know before building something similar.

The code is open source: [https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent](https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent) (sample [analysis](https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent/blob/main/README-1681-HK-2025-12-01.md); overview on [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7400775746727792640/)).

## The Problem

By day I work in data engineering and information security.

Lately, though, I’ve spent my evenings thinking about ways US investors worried about deficits, AI bubbles, and general political instability can hedge those risks by diversifying into mid-cap stocks homed in ex-US markets— markets like Tokyo, Hong Kong, Warsaw, and Kuala Lumpur, where perfectly legitimate, growing, small and mid-cap companies trade and make money without heavy dependence on US markets and trade.

Because equities matching this thesis are often not well covered by US analysts, finding and evaluating them can be hard and takes time that I don’t have. It costs money, too. Bloomberg Terminal is ~$20,000/year. FactSet is just as bad. Yes, you can abandon your portfolio to managers like Fisher, but they will take a cut in return.

With AI now opening up new possibilities, I wondered: Could I build an agentic AI equity research tool on the cheap to help me find and screen equities that fit my thesis and generate easy alpha?

## The Solution I Built

The equity evaluator I ended up building uses LangGraph (an agent orchestration framework) to run a five-phase workflow.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*0lH5NVyc9FUYibKD1RvT-Q.png)

Complete Agentic Graph-Workflow

**Phase 1: Data Gathering (Parallel)**

Four specialist agents run simultaneously:

- **Market Analyst**: price history, liquidity, RSI/MACD/Bollinger
- **Fundamentals Analyst**: P/E, ROE, D/E, cash flow, etc.
- **News Analyst**: recent filings, company news (Tavily)
- **Sentiment Analyst**: retail buzz (StockTwits, Reddit)

This works well for Japan, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and other mature exchanges. It works poorly for Malaysia, Indonesia, and much of Southeast Asia because the free data sources don’t have solid data for listings on exchanges in those regions.

Here’s the actual fallback logic:

```
def get_financial_metrics(ticker: str) -> dict:
    """Try multiple sources, merge results, fill critical gaps."""
    result = {}

    # Primary: yfinance (free, usually good for liquid stocks)
    result = fetch_yfinance(ticker)

    # If key metrics missing, try yahooquery
    if missing_critical_fields(result):
        yahoo_data = fetch_yahooquery(ticker)
        result = merge_keeping_best(result, yahoo_data)

    # Still gaps? Try FMP (if API key available)
    if missing_critical_fields(result) and FMP_API_KEY:
        fmp_data = fetch_fmp(ticker)
        result = merge_keeping_best(result, fmp_data)

    # Last resort: web search for specific missing fields
    if missing_critical_fields(result):
        web_data = search_for_gaps(ticker, result)
        result = merge_keeping_best(result, web_data)

    return sanitize_and_validate(result)
```

The key problem: Searching for some stocks, particularly on exchanges in countries still new to the idea of standard accounting and transparency requirements, returns empty result sets. When that happens, subsequent agents have less to work with.

**Phase 2: Synthesis**

A Research Manager does the best it can with all four reports and writes a summary. In early versions of my code, the output of this stage was a 2,000-plus-word essay. Untethered LLMs can be quite verbose. I reduced the length to a few bullet points through aggressive prompt engineering (“DO NOT write introductory preambles”).

**Phase 3: Bull/Bear Debate (The Transformative Part)**

In this stage, two adversarial agents argue for 1–2 rounds. The Bull gets a prompt that tells it to “argue the STRONGEST case for buying — push back on Bear’s concerns.” The Bear gets instructed to “argue the STRONGEST case for avoiding — and to push back on Bull’s optimism.”

Example from HSBC (0005.HK):

**Bull**: “Trading at P/E 14.79, below historical average. 40% operating margin. $61B in free cash flow. This is cheap, not broken.”

**Bear**: “Revenue growth 4.8% is anemic. EPS declined 16.6% YoY. Operating in Hong Kong during geopolitical uncertainty. Mature bank with no growth story. Hard pass.”

The Portfolio Manager reads both arguments and applies the scoring rules. HSBC in the above case failed the growth requirement → SELL.

This debate-based evaluation structure is better than asking a single LLM to “analyze this stock.” Single agents hedge and compromise. They can also glom onto unintentionally leading language in your prompts. Adversarial agents commit them to different positions and allow subsequent agents to see all aspects of the debate clearly.

**Phase 4: Risk Sizing**

Once the Research Manager is finished synthesizing its inputs, three risk analysts (Conservative, Neutral, and Aggressive) then recommend a position size. Even with prodding, I found that the “Aggressive” one could be pretty cautious — Gemini seems trained to be risk-averse. But that’s OK with me. I’m cautious as well.

**Final Decision**

At this stage, the Portfolio Manager sets up hard gates:

- Financial health rating ≥ 50%
- Growth score ≥ 50%
- Liquidity ≥ $500k daily
- Analyst coverage < 15 (prefer undiscovered names)

HSBC, for example, as of my 2025–11–30 run scored 62.5% on health, 0% on growth → automatic SELL. Samsung scored fine on valuation, but had 100+ analysts talking about it → SELL.

That’s the idea, of course. The system enforces rules I care about, and surfaces only things that don’t break the rules. It saves me time.

In cases like the above, the logic looks like this:

```
def make_decision(health_score, growth_score, liquidity, coverage):
    # Hard fails - any one disqualifies the stock
    if health_score < 0.50:
        return "SELL", "Hard fail: Financial health < 50%"

    if growth_score < 0.50:
        return "SELL", "Hard fail: Growth score < 50%"

    if liquidity < 500_000:
        return "SELL", "Hard fail: Liquidity < $500k"

    if coverage >= 15:
        return "SELL", "Too much analyst coverage (not undiscovered)"

    # Passes all gates - now check soft factors (valuation, risks)
    return assess_qualitative_factors(...)
```

No amount of debate changes these thresholds. If growth = 0%, it’s a SELL.

## Why This Works

**1\. It Enforces Discipline** HSBC looks cheap at P/E 14.79. It’s HSBC — global bank, household name, pays a dividend. A human analyst might think, “But it’s HSBC. I listen to their Banyan Tree podcast. They’re great!” The AI doesn’t care. Growth score: 0%. Rejected.

Would I have rejected HSBC without the system? I don’t know. It might not have even occurred to me to look. Was the system right? Yes, probably. If I’m concerned, I have all the data I need in the report to allow me to start checking.

**2\. The Bull/Bear Structure Forces Deeper Thought** Single-LLM responses are bland and hedged. Multi-agent debate forces each side to commit. It’s the difference between a Wikipedia article (balanced but boring) and a courtroom debate (two strong cases with open advocacy and debate).

**3\. Full Audit Trail** Every data point has a source. Every score shows its calculation. When the system says “Health Score: 62.5% (5/12 raw, adjusted for 8 available points)”, you can see:

- Which 5 points it earned
- Which 7 points were N/A (and why)
- The exact math

Trust comes from being able to verify. Downstream AI agents get to see the work, and so does the user looking at the final report.

**4\. Zero Marginal Cost** After building it, running 100 stocks on Gemini’s free tier costs $0 but the 15 requests per minute limit makes things slow for batch jobs with more than a few tickers. I upgraded to paid second tier for faster batch processing and more generous API limits, and 300 stocks now costs me about $25.

Compare: Bloomberg Terminal is ~$2k/month.

## What Fails Reliably

**1\. Bad Data Contaminates Downstream Results** Case study: YTLPOWR.KL (YTL Power International, Malaysia)

The system on the last run (late Nov 2025) returned:

- P/E Ratio: 161,103,004,000.00 (yes, 161 billion)
- P/B Ratio: 27,230,309,079.61 (27 billion)
- Financial Health Score: 0% (all metrics N/A)

Is YTL Power a bad company? I have no idea. The free APIs don’t cover Malaysian stocks. yfinance scraped corrupted data, FMP returned empty responses, EODHD had nothing on that run (possibly another bug, but most other equities ran just fine).

The system correctly flagged these as anomalous and rejected the stock. But it rejected it for _data gaps_, not because of poor fundamentals. I learned nothing about the actual company.

**Lesson**: Free APIs work well for mature markets. For emerging markets (Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, and many others), you’ll either need paid sources or you’ll have to treat results as low-confidence flags for manual research.

**2\. The System Is Verbose** Early reports were 2,000+ words. For every stock. The Bull agent would write:

“Based on comprehensive analysis of the financial statements, considering both quantitative metrics and qualitative factors, while acknowledging the inherent uncertainty in forward-looking projections…”

I had to add prompts like:

- “DO NOT write introductory preambles”
- “DO NOT summarize your summary”
- “USE bullet points, not paragraphs”
- “Maximum 300 words per response”

Even with these constraints, reports are still 800+ words. LLMs love to hear themselves talk.

**3\. Mega-Caps Sometimes Sneak Through** The thesis: find “undiscovered” stocks (analyst coverage < 15).

This works for mid-caps. For mega-caps the searches aren’t always as helpful:

**Tencent (0700.HK)**: “$400B market cap… but only 12 analysts tracked by yfinance… must be undiscovered!”

Obviously Tencent is not undiscovered. The analyst count is just an artifact of how I’m counting (English-language analysts tracked by yfinance).

**Fix**: I added a market cap threshold (>$50B = automatic “high coverage” assumption). But this is a patch, not a solution. The real issue: the system favors mega-caps because you can _find data on them_. Truly obscure mid-caps in Malaysia exist in data deserts at the moment, and my equity evaluator struggles with them.

**4\. Language Barriers Are Insurmountable** For Korean, Japanese, or Chinese stocks, local sentiment matters. But:

- StockTwits: English-only, US-centric
- Reddit: Mostly English
- Local forums: Require API access and language skills I don’t have

Result: the “Market Sentiment” section for most ex-US stocks says “Unable to assess local sentiment.”

If Samsung is getting roasted on Korean forums, the AI agents may not know about it unless I check manually (which defeats the purpose of having AI agents do the work).

**4\. Memory Isolation Took Longer Than It Should Have** Early versions showed a persistent bug where analyzing Canon would contaminate HSBC’s analysis. The AI would reference Canon’s chip shortage when discussing HSBC (a bank).

The fix:

```
# Before: Global memory (wrong)
bull_memory = ChromaDB(collection="bull_analysis")  # Shared across all tickers
bear_memory = ChromaDB(collection="bear_analysis")
```

```
# After: Ticker-specific namespaces (correct)
def create_memories(ticker: str):
    safe_name = ticker.replace(".", "_")  # 0005.HK → 0005_HK
    return {
        "bull": ChromaDB(collection=f"{safe_name}_bull"),
        "bear": ChromaDB(collection=f"{safe_name}_bear"),
        "risk": ChromaDB(collection=f"{safe_name}_risk")
    }
```

Each ticker now gets isolated memory. Canon’s analysis doesn’t leak into HSBC’s. If you write a system like this, pay close attention to what gets saved and how, and to how and when data gets cleared as it’s generated by the various nodes in the agent graph.

**5\. Speed Is Not a Feature** For 300 tickers:

- Quick mode: 6–12 hours
- Standard mode: 16–30 hours (varied as the code evolved)

Even on quick mode, this is slow. A human with Bloomberg can scan 50 tickers in 30 minutes, and do a passable job.

The trade-off: the AI is thorough (maybe too thorough), but at the moment it’s not fast.

## What I’d Do Differently (Advice)

**1\. Better Data Sources (Optional Future Extension)** I spent 60% of my time on prompt engineering. I should’ve spent 60% finding better data sources.

The difference between “good” and “great” analysis is data quality, not agent architecture.

**For those who want to extend this**: The system is designed to work with free APIs to keep it accessible. But if you’re targeting emerging markets seriously, paid sources would help:

- Polygon.io (international coverage)
- Koyfin ($20/mo for non-professionals)
- Alpha Vantage premium
- Company IR website scrapers (build your own)

The goal is to keep the core system free and open. Paid data should be pluggable extensions, not requirements. If you build connectors for these sources, please, create a pull request — but make sure they’re optional dependencies. Don’t forget to write tests, too, for everything (bad or malicious inputs, broken APIs, dead network connections, timeouts, rate limits, exception handling, and all the things).

**2\. Add a Data Quality Check Before Debate** Before the Bull/Bear debate, add an agent that says:

“8/12 financial metrics available. Data quality: GOOD. Proceed.”

OR

“2/12 financial metrics available. Data quality: POOR. Flag for manual review.”

This would’ve saved me from analyzing YTL Power (the stock with corrupted P/E data of 161 billion).

Implementation:

```
def check_data_quality(metrics: dict) -> str:
    total_fields = 12  # P/E, ROE, ROA, D/E, Revenue, EPS, etc.
    available = sum(1 for v in metrics.values() if v is not None)
    coverage = available / total_fields

    if coverage >= 0.66:  # 8+ of 12 fields
        return "GOOD"
    elif coverage >= 0.33:  # 4-7 of 12 fields
        return "MARGINAL"
    else:  # < 4 fields
        return "POOR"
```

```
# Then in the main flow:
data_quality = check_data_quality(financial_metrics)
if data_quality == "POOR":
    return {
        "decision": "REJECT",
        "reason": "Insufficient data for analysis",
        "metrics_available": f"{available}/{total_fields}"
    }
```

Run this before wasting compute on worthless debates.

**3\. Use Structured Outputs (When Filtering at Scale)** My agents return markdown text. This is human-readable and works well when you’re doing manual research on individual stocks.

**For batch filtering** (screening 300 stocks to find 15 BUYs), I’d love it if someone would add JSON output as an option:

```
{
  "ticker": "0005.HK",
  "company": "HSBC Holdings",
  "decision": "SELL",
  "conviction": "HIGH",
  "health_score": 62.5,
  "growth_score": 0.0,
  "position_size_pct": 0,
  "hard_fails": ["growth_score"],
  "key_risks": ["declining_earnings", "geopolitical_exposure"],
  "rationale": "Passes health gate but fails growth requirement (0% vs 50% threshold)"
}
```

Then we can filter programmatically: `jq '.[] | select(.decision == "BUY")' results.json`.

Keep markdown for detailed reports. Add JSON for programmatic filtering. Both have their place.

**4\. Pick Your Markets Carefully** I wanted a universal ex-US equity analyzer. I learned: there’s no such thing.

- Hong Kong/Japan/Taiwan: Excellent API coverage, system works
- Malaysia/Indonesia/Thailand: Data desert, system struggles
- Korea: Middle ground (decent data, language barrier)
- Most of Europe: solid
- India, Vietnam, Brazil: No access to US retail investors

**Lesson**: Build for the markets you trade. Don’t boil the ocean.

## Is This Useful?

**What this system does well**:

- Filters 300 candidates down to a dozen worth investigating
- Catches many value traps (cheap for a reason)
- Enforces discipline (hard gates prevent emotional trades)
- Costs $0–10 a run instead of $24,000/year

**What it doesn’t do**:

- Replace human judgment (it can’t)
- Work with bad data (garbage in, garbage out)
- Handle emerging markets without paid APIs
- Make real-time trading decisions (it’s too slow)

Think of it like this: if you were hiring an intern to do initial stock screening, this system does that intern’s job. It won’t replace a senior analyst, and it won’t find opportunities that aren’t in the data. But it will save you from wasting time on obvious rejects.

## The Code Is Open

Repository: [https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent](https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent)

**What’s included**:

- Full LangGraph workflow (6 agent types)
- Multi-source data fetcher (yfinance, FMP, EODHD, Tavily)
- 450+ unit tests
- Detailed setup instructions

**Requirements**:

- Python 3.12+
- Google Gemini API key (free tier works)
- Basic command-line skills
- Optional: Tavily, FMP, EODHD keys for better data

**What you can do with it**:

- Screen your own watchlist
- Modify the thesis (change thresholds, add/remove rules)
- Swap in better data sources (please do, but make them optional)
- Study how multi-agent systems work in practice

**What you shouldn’t do**:

- Trust it blindly with real money
- Expect perfection on day one
- Blame me when a recommendation doesn’t work out

## Final Thoughts

The real value of the agentic AI stock evaluator I built isn’t in finding the next 10-bagger. It’s in having a system that says “no” to 285 stocks so I can spend my time on the 15 that matter. That’s worth $100/month (which is what I spent one month).

If you’re building something similar, my advice is: Spend your time on data quality first, and add data confidence checks before you waste compute on garbage inputs. If I were to write it again, I might use structured outputs instead of markdown.

For emerging markets, consider building paid API connectors — but keep them optional so the system remains accessible to everyone at little base cost.

And if you find better free data sources for Malaysia, Indonesia, or Thailand, and other markets with weaker transparency standards, please create a pull request. I’m still looking.

[![](https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRobot/raw/master/figs/logo_white_background.jpg)](https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRobot/blob/master/figs/logo_white_background.jpg) **FinRobot** is an AI Agent platform tailored for financial applications, surpassing FinGPT's single-model approach. It unifies multiple AI technologies—including LLMs, reinforcement learning, and quantitative analytics—to power investment research automation, algorithmic trading strategies, and risk assessment, delivering a full-stack intelligent solution for the financial industry. **Concept of AI Agent**: an AI Agent is an intelligent entity that uses large language models as its brain to perceive its environment, make decisions, and execute actions. Unlike traditional artificial intelligence, AI Agents possess the ability to independently think and utilize tools to progressively achieve given objectives. [Whitepaper of FinRobot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.14767) ![Visitors](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/128b50d501dccf5ec338205f9c8f2b6c272b42aa26076ec711c363db81666b83/68747470733a2f2f6170692e76697369746f7262616467652e696f2f6170692f56697369746f724869743f757365723d41493446696e616e63652d466f756e646174696f6e267265706f3d46696e526f626f7426636f756e74436f6c6f723d25323342313741)[![Discord](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/035ca1b22714e43acc48765667469a3ba7aac29bb7d51a83f98bef7881b8aa0c/68747470733a2f2f646362616467652e6c696d65732e70696e6b2f6170692f7365727665722f747273723853587057353f763d3230323630333230)](https://discord.gg/trsr8SXpW5) ## 🎬 FinRobot Pro — Your Personal AI-Powered Equity Research Assistant [Permalink: 🎬 FinRobot Pro — Your Personal AI-Powered Equity Research Assistant](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#-finrobot-pro--your-personal-ai-powered-equity-research-assistant) 🌐 [https://finrobot.ai/](https://finrobot.ai/) [![](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/31713746/528894248-de3b9f9c-50aa-49f0-82c6-3d2b938f4670.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..UF7lCEwleVWIPGYNwND-qUxiQe-j4VIGS24Bfm5L3GI)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ebgPiJINi-k) ▶️ Click the image above to watch the demo video, or see the short preview below. A locally-deployed AI assistant that fetches financial data, runs multi-agent LLM analysis, and generates professional equity research reports. **1\. Configure API Keys** ``` cp finrobot_equity/core/config/config.ini.example finrobot_equity/core/config/config.ini ``` Edit `config.ini` with your keys: ``` [API_KEYS] fmp_api_key = YOUR_FMP_API_KEY # https://financialmodelingprep.com/developer openai_api_key = YOUR_OPENAI_API_KEY # https://platform.openai.com/account/api-keys adanos_api_key = YOUR_ADANOS_API_KEY # Optional: enables Retail Sentiment Insights ``` **2\. One-Command Deploy (Web Interface)** ``` chmod +x deploy.sh ./deploy.sh start #if deploy.sh not working then python3 -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate pip install -r requirements-equity.txt python run_web_app.py ``` Access at `http://127.0.0.1:8001` | Command | Description | | :-- | :-- | | `./deploy.sh start` | Start the web app (auto-installs dependencies) | | `./deploy.sh stop` | Stop the application | | `./deploy.sh restart` | Restart the application | | `./deploy.sh status` | Check running status | **3\. Or Run via Command Line** ``` # Step 1: Financial analysis python finrobot_equity/core/src/generate_financial_analysis.py \ --company-ticker NVDA \ --company-name "NVIDIA Corporation" \ --config-file finrobot_equity/core/config/config.ini \ --peer-tickers AMD INTC \ --generate-text-sections # Step 2: Generate report python finrobot_equity/core/src/create_equity_report.py \ --company-ticker NVDA \ --company-name "NVIDIA Corporation" \ --analysis-csv output/NVDA/analysis/financial_metrics_and_forecasts.csv \ --ratios-csv output/NVDA/analysis/ratios_raw_data.csv \ --config-file finrobot_equity/core/config/config.ini ``` **Pipeline**: 1. **Fetch Financial Data**: income statements, balance sheets, cash flows via FMP API 2. **Process & Forecast**: 3-year financial projections, DCF valuation, peer comparison 3. **AI Agent Analysis**: 8 specialized agents generate investment thesis, risk assessment, valuation overview, etc. 4. **Report Generation**: professional multi-page HTML/PDF with 15+ chart types ### Example Reports [Permalink: Example Reports](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#example-reports) - [NVDA Equity Research Report](https://ai4finance-foundation.github.io/FinRobot/finrobot_equity/core/output/NVDA_Equity_Research_Report.html) - [MSFT Equity Research Report](https://ai4finance-foundation.github.io/FinRobot/finrobot_equity/core/output/MSFT_Equity_Research_Report.html) - [COP Equity Research Report](https://ai4finance-foundation.github.io/FinRobot/finrobot_equity/core/output/COP_Equity_Research_Report.html) - [TSLA Equity Research Report](https://ai4finance-foundation.github.io/FinRobot/finrobot_equity/core/output/TSLA_Equity_Research_Report.html) - [META Equity Research Report](https://ai4finance-foundation.github.io/FinRobot/finrobot_equity/core/output/META_Equity_Research_Report.html) For full documentation, see [finrobot\_equity/README.md](https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRobot/blob/master/finrobot_equity/README.md). ## What is FinRobot Pro? [Permalink: What is FinRobot Pro?](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#what-is-finrobot-pro) FinRobot-Intro.mp4 [FinRobot Pro](https://finrobot.ai/) is an AI-powered equity research platform that automates professional stock analysis using Large Language Models (LLMs) and AI Agents. **Key Features:** - **Automated Report Generation** – Generate professional equity research reports instantly - **Financial Analysis** – Deep dive into income statements, balance sheets, and cash flows - **Valuation Analysis** – P/E ratio, EV/EBITDA multiples, and peer comparison - **Risk Assessment** – Comprehensive investment risk evaluation ## FinRobot Ecosystem [Permalink: FinRobot Ecosystem](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#finrobot-ecosystem) ![](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/31713746/332781905-6b30d9c1-35e5-4d36-a138-7e2769718f62.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..AHCsJL3jCEMwtu21qJVA9UF-dR8kfKTuJD1IkF94ZCM) ### The overall framework of FinRobot is organized into four distinct layers, each designed to address specific aspects of financial AI processing and application: [Permalink: The overall framework of FinRobot is organized into four distinct layers, each designed to address specific aspects of financial AI processing and application:](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#the-overall-framework-of-finrobot-is-organized-into-four-distinct-layers-each-designed-to-address-specific-aspects-of-financial-ai-processing-and-application) 1. **Financial AI Agents Layer**: The Financial AI Agents Layer now includes Financial Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, enhancing complex analysis and decision-making capacity. Market Forecasting Agents, Document Analysis Agents, and Trading Strategies Agents utilize CoT to dissect financial challenges into logical steps, aligning their advanced algorithms and domain expertise with the evolving dynamics of financial markets for precise, actionable insights. 2. **Financial LLMs Algorithms Layer**: The Financial LLMs Algorithms Layer configures and utilizes specially tuned models tailored to specific domains and global market analysis. 3. **LLMOps and DataOps Layers**: The LLMOps layer implements a multi-source integration strategy that selects the most suitable LLMs for specific financial tasks, utilizing a range of state-of-the-art models. 4. **Multi-source LLM Foundation Models Layer**: This foundational layer supports the plug-and-play functionality of various general and specialized LLMs. ## FinRobot: Agent Workflow [Permalink: FinRobot: Agent Workflow](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#finrobot-agent-workflow) ![](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/31713746/332727895-ff8033be-2326-424a-ac11-17e2c9c4983d.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..Yu24AoZ7D7sadD5n7dv-YX6rgngVjan6lBJkfylTK6k) 1. **Perception**: This module captures and interprets multimodal financial data from market feeds, news, and economic indicators, using sophisticated techniques to structure the data for thorough analysis. 2. **Brain**: Acting as the core processing unit, this module perceives data from the Perception module with LLMs and utilizes Financial Chain-of-Thought (CoT) processes to generate structured instructions. 3. **Action**: This module executes instructions from the Brain module, applying tools to translate analytical insights into actionable outcomes. Actions include trading, portfolio adjustments, generating reports, or sending alerts, thereby actively influencing the financial environment. ## FinRobot: Smart Scheduler [Permalink: FinRobot: Smart Scheduler](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#finrobot-smart-scheduler) ![](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/31713746/332734383-06fa0b78-ac53-48d3-8a6e-98d15386327e.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..dpOLHlHVEi6xOsHXwAxRidjp50ySpRLYYyUXoWsdREI) The Smart Scheduler is central to ensuring model diversity and optimizing the integration and selection of the most appropriate LLM for each task. - **Director Agent**: This component orchestrates the task assignment process, ensuring that tasks are allocated to agents based on their performance metrics and suitability for specific tasks. - **Agent Registration**: Manages the registration and tracks the availability of agents within the system, facilitating an efficient task allocation process. - **Agent Adaptor**: Tailor agent functionalities to specific tasks, enhancing their performance and integration within the overall system. - **Task Manager**: Manages and stores different general and fine-tuned LLMs-based agents tailored for various financial tasks, updated periodically to ensure relevance and efficacy. ## File Structure [Permalink: File Structure](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#file-structure) The main folder **finrobot** has three subfolders **agents, data\_source, functional**. ``` FinRobot ├── finrobot (main folder) │ ├── agents │ ├── agent_library.py │ └── workflow.py │ ├── data_source │ ├── finnhub_utils.py │ ├── finnlp_utils.py │ ├── fmp_utils.py │ ├── sec_utils.py │ └── yfinance_utils.py │ ├── functional │ ├── analyzer.py │ ├── charting.py │ ├── coding.py │ ├── quantitative.py │ ├── reportlab.py │ └── text.py │ ├── toolkits.py │ └── utils.py │ ├── configs ├── experiments ├── tutorials_beginner (hands-on tutorial) │ ├── agent_fingpt_forecaster.ipynb │ └── agent_annual_report.ipynb ├── tutorials_advanced (advanced tutorials for potential finrobot developers) │ ├── agent_trade_strategist.ipynb │ ├── agent_fingpt_forecaster.ipynb │ ├── agent_annual_report.ipynb │ ├── lmm_agent_mplfinance.ipynb │ └── lmm_agent_opt_smacross.ipynb ├── setup.py ├── OAI_CONFIG_LIST_sample ├── config_api_keys_sample ├── requirements.txt └── README.md ``` ## Installation: [Permalink: Installation:](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#installation) **1\. (Recommended) Create a new virtual environment** ``` conda create --name finrobot python=3.10 conda activate finrobot ``` **2\. download the FinRobot repo use terminal or download it manually** ``` git clone https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRobot.git cd FinRobot ``` **3\. install finrobot & dependencies from source or pypi** get our latest release from pypi ``` pip install -U finrobot ``` or install from this repo directly ``` pip install -e . ``` **4\. modify OAI\_CONFIG\_LIST\_sample file** ``` 1) rename OAI_CONFIG_LIST_sample to OAI_CONFIG_LIST 2) remove the four lines of comment within the OAI_CONFIG_LIST file 3) add your own openai api-key ``` **5\. modify config\_api\_keys\_sample file** ``` 1) rename config_api_keys_sample to config_api_keys 2) remove the comment within the config_api_keys file 3) add your own finnhub-api "YOUR_FINNHUB_API_KEY" 4) add your own financialmodelingprep and sec-api keys "YOUR_FMP_API_KEY" and "YOUR_SEC_API_KEY" (for financial report generation) ``` **6\. start navigating the tutorials or the demos below:** ``` # find these notebooks in tutorials 1) agent_annual_report.ipynb 2) agent_fingpt_forecaster.ipynb 3) agent_trade_strategist.ipynb 4) lmm_agent_mplfinance.ipynb 5) lmm_agent_opt_smacross.ipynb ``` ## Demos [Permalink: Demos](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#demos) ### 1\. Market Forecaster Agent (Predict Stock Movements Direction) [Permalink: 1. Market Forecaster Agent (Predict Stock Movements Direction)](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#1-market-forecaster-agent-predict-stock-movements-direction) Takes a company's ticker symbol, recent basic financials, and market news as input and predicts its stock movements. 1. Import ``` import autogen from finrobot.utils import get_current_date, register_keys_from_json from finrobot.agents.workflow import SingleAssistant ``` 2. Config ``` # Read OpenAI API keys from a JSON file llm_config = { "config_list": autogen.config_list_from_json( "../OAI_CONFIG_LIST", filter_dict={"model": ["gpt-4-0125-preview"]}, ), "timeout": 120, "temperature": 0, } # Register FINNHUB API keys register_keys_from_json("../config_api_keys") ``` 3. Run ``` company = "NVDA" assitant = SingleAssistant( "Market_Analyst", llm_config, # set to "ALWAYS" if you want to chat instead of simply receiving the prediciton human_input_mode="NEVER", ) assitant.chat( f"Use all the tools provided to retrieve information available for {company} upon {get_current_date()}. Analyze the positive developments and potential concerns of {company} " "with 2-4 most important factors respectively and keep them concise. Most factors should be inferred from company related news. " f"Then make a rough prediction (e.g. up/down by 2-3%) of the {company} stock price movement for next week. Provide a summary analysis to support your prediction." ) ``` ### 2\. Personal AI Equity Research Assistant (Equity Research Report) [Permalink: 2. Personal AI Equity Research Assistant (Equity Research Report)](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#2-personal-ai-equity-research-assistant-equity-research-report) ### 3\. Trade Strategist Agent with multimodal capabilities [Permalink: 3. Trade Strategist Agent with multimodal capabilities](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#3-trade-strategist-agent-with-multimodal-capabilities) ## AI Agent Papers [Permalink: AI Agent Papers](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#ai-agent-papers) - \[Stanford University + Microsoft Research\] [Agent AI: Surveying the Horizons of Multimodal Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.03568) - \[Stanford University\] [Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.03442) - \[Fudan NLP Group\] [The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.07864) - \[Fudan NLP Group\] [LLM-Agent-Paper-List](https://github.com/WooooDyy/LLM-Agent-Paper-List) - \[Tsinghua University\] [Large Language Models Empowered Agent-based Modeling and Simulation: A Survey and Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.11970) - \[Renmin University\] [A Survey on Large Language Model-based Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.11432.pdf) - \[Nanyang Technological University\] [FinAgent: A Multimodal Foundation Agent for Financial Trading: Tool-Augmented, Diversified, and Generalist](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18485) ## AI Agent Open-Source Frameworks & Tools [Permalink: AI Agent Open-Source Frameworks & Tools](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#ai-agent-open-source-frameworks--tools) - [AutoGPT (183k stars)](https://github.com/Significant-Gravitas/AutoGPT): autonomous AI agent platform. - [Dify (134k stars)](https://github.com/langgenius/dify): LLM app development platform with workflow orchestration and RAG. - [LangChain (130k stars)](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain): framework for building context-aware LLM applications. - [MetaGPT (65.6k stars)](https://github.com/geekan/MetaGPT): multi-agent framework with role-based collaboration. - [AutoGen (56k stars)](https://github.com/microsoft/autogen): framework for multi-agent LLM applications with tools and human interaction. - [CrewAI (46.6k stars)](https://github.com/joaomdmoura/crewAI): framework for orchestrating collaborative AI agents. - [ChatDev (31.7k stars)](https://github.com/OpenBMB/ChatDev): multi-agent framework for software development tasks. - [FastGPT (27.4k stars)](https://github.com/labring/FastGPT): knowledge-based LLM platform with workflow support. - [Langfuse (23.4k stars)](https://github.com/langfuse/langfuse): open-source LLM observability and evaluation platform. - [BabyAGI (22.2k stars)](https://github.com/yoheinakajima/babyagi): task-driven experimental autonomous agent framework. - [SuperAGI (17.3k stars)](https://github.com/TransformerOptimus/SuperAGI): developer-focused autonomous agent framework. - [CAMEL (16.4k stars)](https://github.com/camel-ai/camel): framework for cooperative and communicative AI agents. - [Bisheng (11.2k stars)](https://github.com/dataelement/bisheng): enterprise open-source LLM application platform. ## Citing FinRobot [Permalink: Citing FinRobot](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#citing-finrobot) ``` @article{yang2024finrobot, title={FinRobot: An Open-Source AI Agent Platform for Financial Applications using Large Language Models}, author={Yang, Hongyang and Zhang, Boyu and Wang, Neng and Guo, Cheng and Zhang, Xiaoli and Lin, Likun and Wang, Junlin and Zhou, Tianyu and Guan, Mao and Zhang, Runjia and others}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2405.14767}, year={2024} } @inproceedings{ zhou2024finrobot, title={FinRobot: {AI} Agent for Equity Research and Valuation with Large Language Models}, author={Tianyu Zhou and Pinqiao Wang and Yilin Wu and Hongyang Yang}, booktitle={ICAIF 2024: The 1st Workshop on Large Language Models and Generative AI for Finance}, year={2024} } @inproceedings{han2024enhancing, title={Enhancing Investment Analysis: Optimizing AI-Agent Collaboration in Financial Research}, author={Han, Xuewen and Wang, Neng and Che, Shangkun and Yang, Hongyang and Zhang, Kunpeng and Xu, Sean Xin}, booktitle={ICAIF 2024: Proceedings of the 5th ACM International Conference on AI in Finance}, pages={538--546}, year={2024} } ``` **Disclaimer**: The codes and documents provided herein are released under the Apache-2.0 license. They should not be construed as financial counsel or recommendations for live trading. It is imperative to exercise caution and consult with qualified financial professionals prior to any trading or investment actions. ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/31713746/326045802-e0371951-1ce1-488e-aa25-0992dafcc139.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..Lp_9AYr-oPT5YnsIHEcoUWsG-UlGIknp4JQ7ZzuaLLI) ## About FinRobot: An Open-Source AI Agent Platform for Financial Analysis using LLMs 🚀 🚀 🚀 [finrobot.ai](https://finrobot.ai/ "https://finrobot.ai") ### Topics [finance](https://github.com/topics/finance "Topic: finance") [multimodal-deep-learning](https://github.com/topics/multimodal-deep-learning "Topic: multimodal-deep-learning") [robo-advisor](https://github.com/topics/robo-advisor "Topic: robo-advisor") [large-language-models](https://github.com/topics/large-language-models "Topic: large-language-models") [prompt-engineering](https://github.com/topics/prompt-engineering "Topic: prompt-engineering") [chatgpt](https://github.com/topics/chatgpt "Topic: chatgpt") [fingpt](https://github.com/topics/fingpt "Topic: fingpt") [aiagent](https://github.com/topics/aiagent "Topic: aiagent") ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#readme-ov-file) ### License [Apache-2.0 license](https://github.com/ai4finance-foundation/finrobot#Apache-2.0-1-ov-file) ### Uh oh! web.title: Agentic AI for Finance: Workflows, Tips, and Case Studies
web.description: A practical guide to AI agents in finance. Explore building screening agents, RAG, and multi-agent workflows.
web.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#main-content) notices - See details Notices  Dismiss all # Agentic AI For Finance: Workflows, Tips, and Case Studies **By** [**Brian Pisaneschi, CFA**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/brianpisaneschi) [Table of Contents](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#table-of-contents) ## Introduction Artificial intelligence (AI) agents—once the domain of niche machine-learning teams—have now moved firmly into the investment mainstream. In Q4 2024, earnings call mentions of AI agents jumped 4x quarter-over-quarter, [according to CB Insights](https://www.cbinsights.com/research/ai-agent-trends-to-watch-2025/). Venture capital trends reflect the same momentum. In early-stage hubs like Y Combinator—often a bellwether for the broader venture capital (VC) landscape—agentic AI startups now account for the majority of VC targeting the investment sector.1 Specifically, **Exhibit 1** shows that  over the period January 2024 to June 2025, 73% of the investment-related startups funded by Y Combinator were agentic AI related. ##### **Exhibit 1. Al Classification among Investment-Related Y Combinator Startups (Jan 2024–Jun 2025)** ![ai-agents_exhibit1.jpeg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/jeulohxnlk/jpeg/ai-agents_exhibit1.jpeg?w=800&keep=c&crop=yes&color=cccccc&quality=80&u=bkkbnx) So what is agentic AI? Is the hype leading to value? Many firms have internal proof-of-concept tools swirling around, but can these systems be trusted in production? Specifically, can investment professionals rely on them to make decisions, surface insights, or take other actions? We explore these questions here with practical examples. This installment of [_The Automation Ahead_ series](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series) is a practical guide to Agentic AI in finance. It’s designed to give professionals a clear starting point, grounded in real use cases. You’ll find walkthroughs for the following: - Building a fundamental screening agentic workflow - Running a sustainability research agentic workflow - Combining these into a portfolio construction workflow Along the way, we break down key concepts, common pitfalls, and implementation tips—whether you’re using Python, no-code tools, or both. We close with applied case studies that bring it all together, including a custom index built to client specifications. The goal is to show how these tools, even in their imperfect form, can bring greater nuance, customization, and control to modern investment workflows. Before we get into the how, let’s start with the what: what exactly are AI agents? ## What are Agents? At their core, AI agents are programs that can **independently** take actions on behalf of the user. As large language models (LLMs) have evolved, their ability to perform chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning—where the model breaks problems down step by step—has enabled them to tackle increasingly complex tasks. When these models are trained to output data in formats compatible with traditional automation systems, they gain the ability to call external functions independently. This independence is a key distinction from user-defined LLM **workflows**. _For a deeper understanding of how agentic AI and related technologies fit into broader investment workflows and decision systems, see_ [_AI in Asset Management_](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/foundation/2025/ai-in-asset-management-book) _from CFA Institute Research Foundation._ ### Workflows versus Agents Workflows are **predefined**, step-by-step sequences in which a language model is asked to perform specific tasks—like extracting data, generating summaries, or making classifications—before moving on to the next step. The distinction has led to some confusion between workflows and agents. In fact, due to the novelty of agentic AI systems, a single industry definition of AI agents does not yet exist. For example, Anthropic makes a clear distinction between _workflows_ as predefined code paths and _agents_ as highly autonomous systems2. Whereas [OpenAI](https://cdn.openai.com/business-guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-agents.pdf) makes only a clear distinction between agents and **single-turn LLM** calls, like simple chatbots and sentiment classifiers, the latter not being agents. **Exhibit 2** depicts the differences between LLM systems. ##### **Exhibit 2. LLM Systems** ![workflow_agents.drawio.svg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/ueqqfb8iuh/svg/workflow_agents.drawio.svg?u=bkkbnx) Because finance is a tightly regulated, high-stakes domain, workflow-style automations, which offer more control and predictability, are more likely to be adopted in practice than highly autonomous, low predictability agents. For that reason, this article follows Anthropic’s separation between workflows and agents, with an in-depth focus on workflow patterns relevant to finance use cases. Despite the lack of a single industry definition for agents, consensus is growing around the core building blocks of agentic systems. In the next section, we break down these components and explain how they come together to form agentic AI systems. ### The Building Blocks of an Agent Since AI models are trained on human-generated data, itʼs no surprise that AI agents are built with many of the same functional components we rely on to get things done. To complete a task, we need clear instructions. We use tools to extend our capabilities. We rely on memory to retain context, seek out new information when we donʼt have the answers, and operate within boundaries to avoid mistakes and manage risks. AI agents are no different. **Exhibit 3** shows how the building blocks of an agent work together. ##### **Exhibit 3. Agent Building Blocks** ![agent_components.drawio.svg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/dkz3ntyw1k/svg/agent_components.drawio.svg?u=bkkbnx) We start with the first building block Instructions. #### Instructions Building an agent starts with giving it the right context to act effectively. This is the role of prompt engineering. Well-crafted instructions define what the agent needs to know, what itʼs being asked to do, and how it should do it.3 The following fundamentals shown in **Exhibit 4** outline best practices for writing clear, actionable agent instructions. ##### Exhibit 4. Best Practices for Writing Agent Instructions | Component | What to Include | Example (Fixed-Income Trade-Idea Agent) | | --- | --- | --- | | **Role** | Title + expertise | “Investment-grade Credit Strategist” | | **Task / Objective** | Outcome & success metric | “Produce a 200-word long/short idea with target spread move and catalysts.” | | **Constraints** | Forbidden actions, compliance rules, data limits | - Exclude tickers from \[…\]
- Cite source for each metric
- Abort if spread data > 24h old | | **Guidance** | Tool/agent usage triggers, fallback order, edge-case handling | “Use BloombergAPI first; if error, switch to LSEGAPI . If both fail, return DATA\_STALE .ˮ | | **Response Format** | Schema & style | JSON:\`{"ticker": "...", "direction": "long"} | The table above offers a solid starting point for defining agent instructions—but iteration is essential. In practice, agents often behave in unexpected ways, requiring you to sharpen task definitions, adjust constraints, or clarify assumptions to steer them more effectively. #### Tools LLM tools are just functions you make available for the model to use. You define each tool—usually a name, a short description, and what inputs it needs. When executing a task, the model looks at the available tools. decides if a tool is useful, and then tells your code what inputs to pass. Then, your system runs the tool and sends the result back to the model. This “ [function calling](https://huggingface.co/docs/hugs/en/guides/function-calling)” can be used in several applications, as shown in **Exhibit 5**. ##### Exhibit 5. Tool Types in Finance | Tool Category | What the Agent Can Do | Example Financial Use Case | Example Interfaces | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Data Retrieval** 4 | Fetch third-party data via API endpoints | “Get current P/E ratios for S&P 500” → Calls Bloomberg or FactSet | Bloomberg BQL, LSEG, FactSet | | **Action Execution** 4 | Transform user intent into specific API actions | “Rebalance model portfolio to target weights” → Places trades via broker API | Order Management System, Execution Management System | | **Computation Tasks** 4 | Run math or code to generate output | “Calculate expected return under CAPM” | Python tool, Anthropic/OpenAI code interpreter | | **LLMs as Tools** | Use specialized models or LLMs for specific tasks | “Analyze tone of CEO remarks in Q1 call” → Fine-tuned sentiment LLM or classifier | Hugging Face models | These tools help overcome many of the well-documented limitations of large language models. LLMs arenʼt designed for reliable computation,5  but connecting them to calculators gives them accurate mathematical capabilities. Their knowledge is frozen at the point of their last training cutoff,6  but linking them to application programming interfaces (APIs) and live data feeds restores real-time awareness. Another major limitation is their inherent lack of information that wasn’t available in their training data.7 This leads directly to our next building block: Information Retrieval. #### Information Retrieval For agentic systems to generate accurate, grounded outputs, they must be able to retrieve high-quality, context-specific information on demand. This is where knowledge retrieval comes into play. **Exhibit 6** shows the two primary forms of agentic information retrieval, internal and external. ##### Exhibit 6. Internal and External Information Retrieval | Retrieval Layer | Typical Sources & Method | Common Tools / APIs | | --- | --- | --- | | **Internal** | - Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) - vector databases
- SQL agents hitting proprietary tables | - FAISS, chromadb
- SQL Agents | | **External** | - Web / news search APIs
- “Operator” browser agents that crawl pages and scrape text | Google (SerpAPI), DuckDuckGo, OpenAI/Anthropic search tools | Internal retrieval keeps the LLM grounded on your data. A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) step can surface your internal documents or ground the LLM on more direct information than its general pretrained knowledge. For example, an SQL agent might translate a prompt like “show me the top 10 holdings by VaRˮ into a safe, structured query against your proprietary data warehouse. For a deep dive on RAG, check out the previous installment of the Automation Ahead series, “ [RAG for Finance: Automating Document Analysis with LLMs](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/retrieval-augmented-generation).” External retrieval gives the LLM access to external knowledge sources like the internet. Many platforms are building deep research agents that perform an agentic knowledge retrieval search for a given search topic. First, it generates multiple search queries that explore different angles of the topic. Then, it enters a loop: evaluating the results, deciding whether the information is sufficient, and issuing follow-up queries, if needed. This process continues until the agent determines it has enough context to address the research problem. At that point, it synthesizes the findings and generates a final output—typically in the form of a report or structured summary. Another form of external knowledge retrieval involves “operatorˮ agents that emulate a userʼs behavior in a web browser. These agents use vision-based capabilities to navigate websites, take screenshots, and perform step-by-step actions. The process is iterative: The agent forms a plan, takes control of the browser, observes the screen, and decides the next move based on what it sees. While promising, these agents are still highly experimental and very unstable. Their success depends on factors like page structure, bot detection, loading times, and how well they can interpret visual or interactive content. Often the task could be done much faster by a human. #### Memory Agent memory stores relevant information the system has encountered and may need to recall later—either for its own use or to pass along to another agent or subprocess. Agent memory is typically divided into two forms: - **Short-term memory** holds immediate context like conversation history or the current process state. This allows information to persist across steps and is often represented as structured datasets, lists, or key-value pairs. - **Long-term memory** captures persistent knowledge accumulated over time. This usually involves writing important data to a vector database, which can be queried through a RAG system to surface relevant context on demand. In practice, most agents use a hybrid memory system that combines both short- and long-term memory, depending on the complexity and duration of the task at hand. #### Guardrails Guardrails act as the agentʼs defense system. They prevent misuse, limit risk, and ensure outputs stay aligned with user intent and institutional constraints. This can include blocking off-topic or malicious inputs, enforcing tool usage limits, or escalating actions to a human when thresholds are exceeded. **Exhibit 7** presents a breakdown of the agent’s key guardrail layers that form its defense system. ##### Exhibit 7. Key Guardrail Layers | Layer | What It Does | Example | | --- | --- | --- | | **System Prompt** | Establishes core behavioral rules (“cite every sourceˮ) | Included at the start of every LLM thread | | **Input Filters** | Block or sanitize user prompts or LLM outputs that violate scope or policy | A relevance classifier rejects “tell me a joke” | | **Tool-Call Gatekeepers** | Validate or throttle how the LLM calls external functions | Prevent trades larger than 5% of portfolio | | **Output Checks** | Scan drafts for restricted terms, entities, or compliance risks | Block responses containing 'CONFIDENTIAL' labels | | **Human-in-the-Loop** | Requires manual approval for high-impact or sensitive actions | A trader must approve before an order is executed | Each layer plays a distinct role in securing agentic systems. It starts with a system prompt, which sets nonnegotiables up front—like always citing sources. Input filters screen out irrelevant or harmful queries before they reach the model, while output checks scan responses for red flags like the presence of bias, personally identifiable information, or brand-risk language. Sitting between input and output, tool-call gatekeepers enforce hard constraints on how tools are used—for example, setting maximum trade sizes or limiting how often the system can call an external API (rate limiting). And when real money or risk is involved, a human-in-the-loop layer gives the final say before execution. You might wonder what separates guardrails from constraints defined in the instructions. In practice, the two arenʼt mutually exclusive. Constraints written into the system prompt serve as text-based guardrails:  They define expected behavior and limitations for the LLM. But guardrails also include programmatic controls, such as functions, filters, or workflow logic that actively monitor, validate, or block the modelʼs inputs and outputs. A well-guarded agentic system puts guardrails into the entire workflow, adding more reliability, control, and compliance at every step. The components above form the core infrastructure of an agentic system. But building true workflows requires more than assembling parts: It requires decomposing complex tasks into structured, controllable steps. In the next section, we explore common agentic workflow patterns and how they apply to investment use cases. ### Workflows in Finance Since the key distinction between LLM calls, workflows, and agents is the level of autonomy involved, itʼs useful to break them down along that spectrum. Weʼll start with the most structured, least autonomous systems and work our way up to more complex, agentic architectures. Keep in mind that the following workflows fall under the umbrella of agentic AI. In the next section, “ [Agents in Finance](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#Agents-in-Finance),” we explore the more autonomous end of this spectrum—AI agents and how those systems apply to finance. #### Prompt Chaining Prompt chaining breaks a complex task into a **linear sequence** of LLM calls, where each step builds on the output of the previous one. The task is divided into sub-tasks, with each sub-task output feeding into the next. You can also insert validation steps to decide whether the chain should continue, branch, or stop. At any point in the chain, tools can be invoked to enhance accuracy or retrieve external data. **Exhibit 8** depicts the prompt-chaining process. ##### **Exhibit 8. Calculate ratios → 3. Filter by thresholds → 4. Return ranked list → 5.
