1.5 %, turnover < 20 bps | All risk checks pass or 5 optimization rounds completed | The workflow patterns above offer a foundation for building agentic systems, but theyʼre just the starting point. The flexibility of agentic AI allows for endless combinations, and real-world tasks often require custom stacking and adaptation of these patterns. Next, we explore more autonomous agent systems that push beyond predefined flows and begin to reason, plan, and act with greater independence. ### Agents in Finance As reasoning models have advanced, LLMs have improved their ability to generate well-structured plans. Through techniques like chain-of-thought prompting, models are now better at thinking step by step—mapping out their reasoning before taking action. When this capability is paired with the agentic building blocks outlined earlier—tools, memory, retrieval, and guardrails—LLMs can move beyond passive responses and begin to actively execute on tasks. This shift lays the foundation for one of the most popular agentic frameworks to date: the **ReAct** framework. #### ReAct The ReAct paradigm (short for **Reason + Act**) prompts a model to emit two outputs: a chain-of-thought explaining why it is doing something and a concrete action describing what tool to call next. This cyclical process is depicted in Exhibit 18.  In their [original 2022 paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), Yao, Zhao, Yu, Du, Shafran, Narasimhan, and Cao  demonstrated that this approach reduces hallucinations and enables a single LLM to plan, fetch data, validate results, and iterate until the task is complete. ##### **Exhibit 18. ReAct Cycle** ![react_pattern.drawio.svg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/rdvwvbjft7/svg/react_pattern.drawio.svg?u=bkkbnx) Here is an example of what the ReAct reasoning trace might look like: > \> Question: Is AT&T’s current dividend payout sustainable over the next two years? > > Thought: I need free cash flow (FCF) and total dividends paid to assess coverage. First I'll get the Trailing 12-month(TTM) Free Cashflow for AT&T with ticker T > > Action: FinDataAPI.get\_metric("T", "FCF", "TTM") > > Observation: 27.4 B > > Thought: Now I’ll fetch dividends paid over the same period. > > Action: FinDataAPI.get\_metric("T", "DividendsPaid", "TTM") > > Observation: 14.8 B > > Thought: I’ll calculate the coverage ratio (FCF / DividendsPaid). > > Action: Calculator.calc("27.4 / 14.8") > > Observation: ≈ 1.85 > > Thought: A ratio above 1.0 suggests the payout is currently covered. Let's project FCF forward  2 years under modest growth assumptions. > > Action: Calculator.calc("27.4 \* (1 + 0.02)^2") > > Observation: ≈ 28.5 B > > Thought: If dividends remain flat at ~14.8 B, coverage stays strong. > > Action: Calculator.calc("28.5 / 14.8") > > Observation: ≈ 1.93 > > Thought: Coverage remains well above 1.5x, even under flat-to-modest growth. > > Final Answer: Based on trailing FCF of $27.4B and dividends of $14.8B (coverage ≈ 1.85×), plus forward estimates, AT&T’s dividend appears sustainable for at least the next two years. Key risks include rising capex or interest expenses. This pattern is the foundation of many modern autonomous agents: a single LLM reasoning step by step, calling tools, and adjusting as it goes. Often though, the system can benefit from more than one specialized agent working in tandem—sharing information and coordinating toward a shared goal. That’s where multi-agent frameworks come in. #### Multi-Agent Orchestration A multi-agent system swaps a single, all-purpose model for a coordinated team of specialists—each with its own role, tools, and objectives. At the center sits an orchestrator responsible for routing tasks, managing handoffs, and consolidating results. As **Exhibit 19** shows, the orchestrator assigns subtasks to the appropriate agents, passes data and outputs between them, and oversees tool usage across the system. Like a project lead, it runs its own ReAct-style reasoning loop—evaluating observations, determining next steps, and deciding when the overall task is complete. ##### **Exhibit 19. Multi-Agent Orchestration** ![multi-agent_orchestration.drawio.svg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/qeqdse8nto/svg/multi-agent_orchestration.drawio.svg?u=bkkbnx) **When multi-agent beats single-agent** - Tasks break naturally into domain roles (data pull, modeling, narrative, compliance) - Sub-tasks can run in parallel - Different agents need different LLMs or tool sets (e.g., a cheap classifier vs. a reasoning-heavy model) - You want clear ownership for audit—each agentʼs log shows who did what We now transition to the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a widely used approach for enabling autonomous agents to coordinate, share context, and interact with tools or each other in a structured way. #### Model Context Protocol Model Context Protocol is emerging as the de facto plumbing layer for autonomous-agent systems. An MCP server sits in front of an existing API (market-data feed, CRM, SQL backend) and presents a uniform, LLM-friendly interface—so an agent can discover the endpoint, see the input schema, and call it safely without hard-coded function or custom prompts. Originally proposed by Anthropic8 as a way to standardize “how LLMs talk to external toolsˮ, MCP is now supported—at least experimentally—by OpenAI, Google, and several open-source stacks. The attraction is speed: Once an API is wrapped in MCP, any compliant agent framework can consume it with near-zero code. No-code orchestration platforms, such as n8n and Zapier, have already added MCP connectors, instantly exposing their large catalogs of SaaS integrations to tool-using LLMs. ### When to Use Agents versus Workflows? Before choosing a build pattern, ask a single question: “Can I write the exact sequence of steps up front?” If the answer is _yes_, a traditional workflow—prompt chaining, routing, or an orchestrator–worker pattern—delivers speed, predictability, and a simple audit trail. If the answer is _no_, if the task can deal with variability in outputs, and if it lies in a lower risk camp, consider using an agent. But do not underestimate the higher costs of LLM inference, as the looping can become quite costly, especially with higher cost reasoning models. **Exhibit 20** shows examples of how to choose between build patterns. ##### Exhibit 20. When to Use Agents vs. Workflows | Pattern to Pick | Typical Task Profile | What You Optimize for | Task Riskiness | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Workflow** | Known steps, repeatable logic, low ambiguity | Speed, determinism, auditability | High | | **Agent** | Steps are unclear or evolve | Flexibility, exploration | Low | Todayʼs agents are the least capable weʼll ever use. They will undoubtedly evolve to handle higher-risk tasks with greater reliability. Still, the need for transparent, deterministic systems means predefined workflows will likely remain essential for production use cases for the foreseeable future in finance. ## Conclusion Agentic AI is on track to influence every part of modern organizations. Given the high-risk nature of many investment workflows, we have a responsibility to build systems that are transparent, controllable, and aligned with institutional standards. Now is the time to experiment, test boundaries, and learn—while the stakes are still low enough to do so safely. This article outlined the foundational building blocks of agentic AI systems, explored practical workflow patterns, and offered a path toward turning early prototypes into more reliable, auditable tools. In the case studies that follow, we offer more in-depth examples of how you can build valuable agentic AI systems even in their imperfect state. We also encourage those interested in building these systems to follow along with the code on the [RPC Labs GitHub page](https://github.com/CFA-Institute-RPC/The-Automation-Ahead) and we welcome contributions for implementations of finance-specific agentic AI implementation. ### Case Study 1: Fundamental Assessment Agentic Workflow This agentic workflow is designed to assess whether current economic conditions are favorable for a specific company based on fundamental financial criteria. It introduces an economic regime-aware screening process with human-in-the-loop inputs, as shown in **Exhibit A1**. This use case demonstrates how reasoning-capable LLM agents can blend macroeconomic awareness, financial data retrieval, and conditional logic to generate informed assessments. ##### **Exhibit A1. Economic Regime-Based Fundamental Assessment Router Workflow** ![economic_regime_fundamentals.drawio.svg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/cdlnqfkvnt/svg/economic_regime_fundamentals.drawio.svg?u=bkkbnx) ### Workflow Summary This use case follows a combined **Promt-Chaining** and **Router workflow pattern** with regime-specific branching. It can be broken down into the following steps: 1. **Get Ticker** 2. **Pull Financial Data** 3. **Route to Economic Regime** 4. **Calculate Regime-Specific Metrics** 5. **Provide Data Validation Commentary** 6. **Generate Scored Assessment** Each step can be more or less autonomous, depending on user preference. **Click the steps to dive in.** - [Step 1: Get Ticker](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2349443464) - [Step 2: Pull Financial Data](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1010350550) - [Step 3: Route to Economic Regime](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1977282117) - [Step 4: Calculate Regime-Specific Metrics](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1736708336) - [Step 5: Provide Data Validation Commentary](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-446932838) - [Step 6: Generate Scored Assessment](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1376932270) [Step 1: Get Ticker](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2349443464) The workflow begins by the user inputting the ticker for analysis. [Step 2: Pull Financial Data](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1010350550) The system fetches comprehensive financial data (annual/quarterly income statements and balance sheets) for the target company. [Step 3: Route to Economic Regime](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1977282117) The user selects from four predefined economic regimes: - **0 = Expansionary:** Focus on growth and leverage metrics - **1 = Inflationary:** Focus on pricing power and inventory efficiency - **2 = Stagflationary:** Focus on margin stability and low leverage - **3 = Recession:** Focus on liquidity and defensive positioning **Example:** The user selects **2 = Stagflationary** environment. [Step 4: Calculate Regime-Specific Metrics](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1736708336) Based on the selected regime, the workflow branches to calculate specific financial metrics. For stagflation, the system calculates the metrics shown in **Exhibit A2**. **Exhibit A2. Stagflationary Environment Metrics** | Metric | Favorable | Neutral | Unfavorable | Rationale | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **EBITDA Margin Volatility (stdev)** | < 4% | 4% – 6% | \> 6% | Operational stability matters when growth slows and costs rise | | **Gross Margin Trend (YoY, bps)** | \> +50 | 0 to +50 | < 0 | Ability to hold or expand gross margin indicates pricing power | | **Net Debt / EBITDA** | < 1.0x | 1.0x–2.0x | \> 2.0x | Low leverage mitigates refinancing and spread-widening risk | These thresholds can be stored for different economic regimes for the agent to retrieve during evaluation and based on the confirmed regime. [Step 5: Provide Data Validation Commentary](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-446932838) The system validates calculated metrics against predefined outlier bounds for the given metrics to identify potential data anomalies. **Example Prompt:** ```python Copy prompt = f"""Evaluate the following financial metrics for accuracy, completeness, and nuance. {metrics)} Look at the outlier table: {OUTLIER_TABLE} -Comment on any metrics that fall in the extreme upper and lower bounds. -Determine if the metrics are reasonable based on the input data or if there are odd/missing items that are affecting their calculations. Input data: Annual Income Statement:{ann_inc} Quarterly Income Statement:{qtr_inc} Annual Balance Sheet:{ann_bs} Quarterly Balance Sheet:{qtr_bs} -Lastly, comment on the nuance that may be affecting these metrics that would be important to determine their value. For example, if gross margin volatility is in the extreme upper bound but the volatility is mostly coming from the upside or extremely high values of gross margin then provide this nuance in the commentary.""" ``` The resulting metric outlier bounds are then shown in **Exhibit A3**. ##### Exhibit A3. Metric Outlier Bounds | Metric | Q1 | Q3 | IQR | Extreme Lower Bound (Q1 −
1.5×IQR) | Extreme Upper Bound (Q3 +
1.5×IQR) | Notes | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **EBITDA Margin  Volatility - Annual (std)** | 1.0% | 5.0% | 4.0% | −5.0% (floor 0) | 11.0% | Excessive volatility suggests instability; near-zero (if seen) might prompt sanity check | | **Gross Margin Trend (YoY, bps)** | -100 | +100 | 200 bps | −400 bps | +400 bps | Large swings could reflect mix, pricing, or recognition timing anomalies | | **Net Debt / EBITDA** | 1.0x | 4.0x | 3.0x | −3.5x | 8.5x | High leverage or large net cash (negative) should be validated for definitions and normalization | **Sample Output (condensed for readability)** ```plaintext Copy Let's evaluate each of the three key metrics provided — **EBITDA Margin Volatility**, **Gross Margin Trend (YoY, bps)**, and **Net Debt / EBITDA** — for accuracy, completeness, and nuance, referencing the input data and the outlier table bounds. ## 1. EBITDA Margin Volatility - **Reported Metric:** 0.0085 (or 0.85%) - **Outlier Table Bounds:** - Extreme Lower Bound: 0% (floor) - Extreme Upper Bound: 11.0% ### Data Review: - Annual EBITDA Margins: - 2021: 14.93% - 2022: 13.06% - 2023: 14.48% - 2024: 13.56% ### Assessment: - Volatility is **below Q1**, reflecting **very stable margins**. - Fluctuations are modest (~13–15%) and consistent. - No data issues observed. - **Nuance:** - Low volatility indicates operational consistency. ## 2. Gross Margin Trend (YoY, bps) - **Reported Metric:** –45.33 bps - **Outlier Table Bounds:** - Extreme Lower Bound: -400 bps - Extreme Upper Bound: +400 bps ### Data Review: - Gross Margins: - 2021: 21.24% - 2022: 19.39% - 2023: 18.55% - 2024: 19.88% - YoY Changes: - 2022: –1.85% - 2023: –0.84% - 2024: +1.34% - Quarterly trend avg: –16.63 bps ### Assessment: - The trend is **within bounds**, showing a moderate overall decline. - Quarterly margins range from 21.33% (Q2) to 19.31% (Q4) in 2024. - **Nuance:** - Trend reflects earlier declines partially offset in 2024. - Cost pressures and revenue mix shifts likely drivers. ## 3. Net Debt / EBITDA - **Reported Metric (2024):** 3.30x - **Outlier Table Bounds:** - Extreme Lower Bound: 0 - Extreme Upper Bound: 8.5x ### Data Review: - Net Debt: - 2021: 5.44B → 2024: 6.11B - EBITDA: - 2021: 1.92B → 2024: 1.85B - Ratio: - 2021: 2.83x → 2024: 3.30x ### Assessment: - **Within bounds**, but trending upward. - Increase driven by declining EBITDA, not rising debt. - **Nuance:** - EBITDA decline tied to unusual items and lower ops income. - Debt stable, but total debt grew slightly. - Cash is low (~588M) relative to 7.19B total debt. - Interest expense (~348M) is high; coverage should be monitored. ## Additional Observations and Nuances ### Unusual Items and Their Impact - Recurring restructuring and M&A charges (e.g., –97M in 2024) affect EBITDA and margins. - Normalized EBITDA is slightly higher; more reflective of core performance. ### Seasonality and Quarterly Fluctuations - Quarterly EBITDA and gross margins show variation. - Q2 2024 margins notably stronger. - Indicates some seasonality or timing effects. # **Final Conclusion** - **All three metrics are reasonable and aligned with the data.** - **No outliers or red flags.** - Unusual items introduce some noise, so **normalized metrics are more informative**. - **EBITDA margin volatility is low**, but plausible. - **Gross margin trend shows moderate decline**, with some recent improvement. - **Net debt / EBITDA is moderate**, but upward trend and liquidity limits warrant monitoring. ``` [Step 6: Generate Scored Assessment](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1376932270) The system uses the calculated metrics and then cross-references this against the screening criteria for the regime. Based on the outcome of the metric assessment and the data validation commentary, the LLM generates a score and an explanation for the score. **Sample Output** ```plaintext Copy Explanation: The evaluation of the three key financial metrics against the criteria for a Stagflationary regime shows the following: 1. EBITDA Margin Volatility is very low at 0.85%, below the first quartile bound of 1%, indicating stable operational profitability with minimal variability. This is consistent with the relatively stable EBITDA margins over the past four years. While low volatility is plausible, it should be sanity checked to ensure no smoothing accounting adjustments mask true variability. 2. Gross Margin Trend (YoY) is negative at −45 bps, within the acceptable bounds (−100 to +100 bps). The trend reflects a moderate decline in gross margin from 2021 to 2023, with some recovery in 2024. This suggests operational challenges and cost pressures typical in a stagflationary environment, but the decline is not extreme. 3. Net Debt / EBITDA ratio is 3.30x, within the interquartile range but approaching the upper quartile bound of 4.0x. The increase from 2.83x in 2021 to 3.30x in 2024 is driven by stable net debt and declining EBITDA in 2024. This moderate leverage increase warrants monitoring, especially given limited liquidity buffers and significant interest expenses. Additional nuances include the impact of unusual items and restructuring charges that affect EBITDA and margins, suggesting normalized metrics should be prioritized. Quarterly fluctuations and seasonality also influence margin trends. Overall, the metrics are reasonable, consistent with the data, and reflect characteristics expected in a stagflationary regime: stable but low margin volatility, moderate margin pressure, and increasing leverage. No extreme outliers or data errors are evident. Score: 85 ``` For a code implementation of this example, follow the tutorial available [here](https://github.com/CFA-Institute-RPC/The-Automation-Ahead/tree/main/Agents/regime_screening_workflow). ### Case Study 2: Deep Research Sustainability Screening Workflow This agent uses a hybrid **Parallelization** and **Evaluator–Optimizer** workflow pattern to perform deep research on a company’s adoption of innovative sustainable technologies. It enables investment teams to assess sustainability leadership using dynamic, internet-scale data. #### Objective The user inputs a prompt like the following: > Assess and score Company X’s adoption of innovative sustainable technology. Provide a score from 0–100 based on the following five dimensions. The five evaluation criteria include: > > 1. **Key Technologies Adopted** – What sustainable technologies has the company implemented? > 2. **Innovation Leadership** – Are there any cutting-edge initiatives, R&D efforts, or partnerships? > 3. **Measurable Impact** – Are there quantifiable environmental or business outcomes? > 4. **Future Strategy** – What are the documented future commitments or roadmaps? > 5. **Industry Position** – How does the company compare to its competitors? #### Workflow Structure The workflow structure for this case is shown in **Exhibit A4**. ##### **Exhibit A4. Deep Research Sustainability Screening Agentic Workflow** ![sustainability_screening.drawio.svg](https://cfainstitute.widen.net/content/qhsmrq1bsa/svg/sustainability_screening.drawio.svg?u=bkkbnx) - [1\. Query Planning](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-734931860) - [2\. Worker Execution (Parallel Search and Extraction)](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1090430047) - [3\. Evaluation Loop (Evaluator–Optimizer)](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-3530175621) - [4\. Synthesis and Scoring](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-4282741810) - [Example Output (Excerpt)](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2119482239) [1\. Query Planning](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-734931860) The first LLM receives the user’s prompt and generates targeted search queries for each of the five evaluation criteria—for example: - "Company X sustainable technology adoption 2025" - "Company X sustainability R&D initiatives" - "Company X environmental impact from sustainability programs" - "Company X climate strategy roadmap" - "Company X sustainability leadership vs. peers 2025" [2\. Worker Execution (Parallel Search and Extraction)](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-1090430047) Each query is sent to a search agent that: - Conducts an internet search and - Extracts and summarizes relevant content This is done in parallel to reduce latency. [3\. Evaluation Loop (Evaluator–Optimizer)](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-3530175621) The results are sent to an evaluator which evaluates the quality and completeness of the results: - If coverage is incomplete, it generates follow-up queries. - This loop repeats until the system reaches a predefined confidence threshold that enough information has been gathered to score the company meaningfully. [4\. Synthesis and Scoring](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-4282741810) Once sufficient information is gathered, the results are compiled into a report: - Assigns sub-scores for each evaluation criterion - Justifies each score with extracted evidence - Aggregates to a final score between 0–100 - Outputs a final narrative explaining the rating [Example Output (Excerpt)](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2119482239) > Final Score: 82/100 > > Company X has demonstrated strong adoption of innovative sustainable technologies. Notable highlights include: > > - Adoption of carbon capture solutions in two facilities (Key Tech Score: 16/20) > - Participation in a joint R&D lab with a major university on green hydrogen (Innovation Leadership: 18/20) > - A reported 12% reduction in operational emissions since 2022 (Impact: 15/20) > - A public commitment to net-zero Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 2035 (Strategy: 17/20) > - Sustainability rankings place Company X in the top quartile of its sector (Position: 16/20) [Notes](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#notes) This kind of workflow is highly extensible. Users could add custom weights to the scoring dimensions or adjust scoring rubrics depending on strategy mandates (e.g., thematic ESG funds, transition-focused portfolios, etc.). For a code implementation of this example, follow the tutorial [here.](https://github.com/CFA-Institute-RPC/The-Automation-Ahead/tree/main/Agents/sustainability_research_workflow) In the next case, we connect this workflow into a broader portfolio construction use case that builds and tailors custom indexes using the workflows we introduced above. ### Case Study 3: Agentic AI-Augmented Portfolio Construction for Custom Dividend Strategy This case study shows how traditional portfolio construction techniques can be augmented with agentic systems to produce highly customized, client-specific portfolios at scale. The agent-enhanced process combines traditional fundamental factor models, agentic sustainability scoring, and agentic fundamental screening to create a bespoke equity portfolio. #### Task Scope A wealth manager is asked to build a custom, separately managed account (SMA) for a high-net-worth client. The client requests the following: - A portfolio of US companies drawn from the S&P 500 - A dividend yield 20% higher than the S&P 500 - A beta between 0.8 and 1.2 - Sustainability considerations—adoption of innovative sustainable technology - Benchmark: S&P Global Dividend Aristocrats Index - [Step 1: Define the Universe and Extract Data](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-3042780025) - [Step 2: Construct Dividend Factors](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2469224591) - [Step 3: Run Agentic AI-Based Sustainability Screen](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2577250370) - [Step 4: Run Regime-Based Fundamental Assessment](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2875933584) - [Step 5: Final Composite Ranking](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-129070516) - [Step 6: Portfolio Optimization](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2976689673) - [Outcome](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-3138560699) [Step 1: Define the Universe and Extract Data](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-3042780025) The base universe is the **S&P 500**. Data are extracted for each company: - **Dividend Yield** - **Payout Ratio** (as a proxy for dividend sustainability) - **3-Year Dividend Growth Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** - **Price History** (for return calculation) - **Sector Classification** [Step 2: Construct Dividend Factors](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2469224591) We use a decile spread methodology to construct three dividend factors, comprising dividend yield, payout ratio, and dividend growth rate (3-year CAGR). The methodology includes taking the returns of companies in the top decile of the given factor and subtracting the returns of companies in the bottom decile of the factor. Each factor is regressed on the returns of each company, creating beta values representing the exposure to each factor. Given we want to rank high for each factor, we use a rank methodology to score each company for a given factor (i.e., the highest factor is ranked 1, and the lowest factor is ranked 100). These scores are summed to create a **composite dividend factor score** (see **Exhibit A5**), and the top 100 companies are selected (see **Exhibit A6**). ##### Exhibit A5. Example Factor Table | Company | Dividend Yield Beta | Payout Ratio Beta | Dividend Growth Beta | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Company C | 0.91 | 0.79 | 0.88 | | Company D | 0.76 | 0.91 | 0.92 | | Company A | 0.82 | 0.63 | 0.77 | | Company B | 0.67 | 0.85 | 0.69 | | Company E | 0.89 | 0.73 | 0.65 | ##### Exhibit A6. Example Composite Rank | Company | Dividend Yield Rank | Payout Ratio Rank | Dividend Growth Rank | Composite Score | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Company C | 1 | 3 | 2 | 6 | | Company D | 4 | 1 | 1 | 6 | | Company A | 3 | 5 | 3 | 11 | | Company B | 5 | 2 | 4 | 11 | | Company E | 2 | 4 | 5 | 11 | [Step 3: Run Agentic AI-Based Sustainability Screen](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2577250370) Each of the top 100 companies is evaluated by the **Deep Research Sustainability Workflow**, which scores them on a 0–100 scale based on the following factors: - Adoption of sustainable technology - Innovation leadership - Measurable impact - Future strategy - Industry position The scores are normalized and added as a fourth factor in the composite rank. [Step 4: Run Regime-Based Fundamental Assessment](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2875933584) Next, the **Economic Regime Workflow** is activated to evaluate how well each company is positioned for the current macroeconomic environment (e.g., stagflation, inflation, recession, expansion). These scores are added as a fifth factor. [Step 5: Final Composite Ranking](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-129070516) All five signals are combined into a **final composite rank:** - Dividend factor scores - Sustainability screen score - Economic regime alignment score The top 50 companies by composite rank are selected for portfolio construction. **Exhibit A7** shows an example of a final composite rank matrix. ##### Exhibit A7. Example Final Composite Rank Matrix | Company | Dividend Yield Rank | Payout Ratio Rank | Dividend Growth Rank | Sustainability Rank | Regime Fit Rank | Composite Score | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Company C | 1 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 9 | | Company D | 4 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 11 | | Company A | 3 | 5 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 15 | | Company E | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 20 | | Company B | 5 | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 20 | [Step 6: Portfolio Optimization](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-2976689673) A traditional optimizer is used to generate the final portfolio. Constraints include the following: - **Objective:** Maximize dividend yield - **Beta Target:** Between 0.8 and 1.2 - **Sector Deviation:** ±5% of S&P 500 sector weights - **Position Limits:** Max 5% per position [Outcome](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#4257225834-3138560699) The resulting portfolio is: - aligned to the client's income objective and constraints and - tailored using interpretable sustainability, macroeconomic and fundamental filters. This approach highlights how agentic systems can replace rigid filters with dynamic assessments. Rather than relying on hard cutoffs, agentic AI-generated scores allow for ranking-based filtering, which is far more adaptable to large universes or when clients request customization across multiple dimensions. For a code implementation of this example, follow the tutorial available [here](https://github.com/CFA-Institute-RPC/The-Automation-Ahead/blob/main/Agents/Agent-Augmented%20Portfolio%20Construction%20-%20Case%20Study.ipynb). ### Challenges and Tips #### Choosing the Right Models Choosing the right models starts with aligning their capabilities to the task. High-context reasoning—like building a cash flow model or interpreting legal covenants—warrants a capable reasoning model like OpenAIʼs [o-series](https://openai.com/index/introducing-o3-and-o4-mini/) models. But for routine steps like routing, classification, or simple extraction, non-reasoning models or even fine-tuned small language models (SMLs) can handle the job efficiently, saving on both cost and latency. In mixed-agent workflows, you can assign models strategically: Use heavyweight models for synthesis and decision making, and delegate repetitive or narrow tasks to lighter models. Context length also plays a role. Processing a 10-K filing or board presentation may require a 100k-token window or a RAG layer, while a simple routing prompt wonʼt. #### Output Variability Output variability is an inherent feature of stochastic text generation. One of the simplest ways to reduce it is by lowering the temperature parameter, which controls the degree of randomness in model outputs. This can improve determinism in sections where consistency is important. However, variability can still appear even at a temperature of zero. In fact, OpenAI has removed the temperature setting entirely for its reasoning models. When the task requires a high degree of consistency between runs—such as with scoring agents— it is best to either use a non-reasoning model with temperature set to zero or apply a majority-vote approach to approximate limiting behavior. Another source of variability arises when a model incorporates live internet data, since the retrieved context can change between runs. A Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline offers a more controlled alternative: instead of letting the model fetch dynamic web content, it is anchored to curated documents or structured datasets, which reduces both randomness and hallucinations. #### Debugging and Evaluation As you stack tools and agents, complexity can escalate quickly—especially in multi-agent systems. Default to simplicity. Start by testing each component in isolation. Confirm the SQL agent generates safe queries, the risk model returns coherent outputs, and the PDF renderer handles edge cases—all before wiring them together. Instrument your workflows with logging at every step: agent inputs and outputs, tool calls, API responses, and edge-case branches. Without detailed logs, diagnosing failures becomes guesswork. Simplicity, paired with robust observability, is your best defense against fragile agentic systems. Taken together, these practices move a prototype closer to something reliable, auditable, and resilient—the foundations needed to turn promising proof of concepts into real, measurable value. [Footnotes](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/the-automation-ahead-content-series/agentic-ai-for-finance#footnotes) \[1\] See the Y Combinator Startup Directory: [https://www.ycombinator.com/companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/companies). \[2\] Anthropic, “Building Effective Agents” (19 December 2024): [https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents). \[3\] Microsoft Copilot Studio, “Instructive Guidance for Generative Orchestration” (8 July 2025): [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-copilot-studio/guidance/generative-mode-guidance](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-copilot-studio/guidance/generative-mode-guidance). \[4\] Hugging Face: [https://huggingface.co/docs/hugs/en/guides/function-calling](https://huggingface.co/docs/hugs/en/guides/function-calling). \[5\] A. Patel, S. Bhattamishra, and N. Goyal, “Are NLP Models Really Able to Solve Simple Math Word Problems?” ACL Anthology (June 2021): [https://aclanthology.org/2021.naacl-main.168/](https://aclanthology.org/2021.naacl-main.168/). \[6\] M. Komeili, K. Shuster, and J. Weston, “Internet-Augmented Dialogue Generation,” ACL Anthology (May 2022): [https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.579/](https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.579/). \[7\] J. Maynez, S. Narayan, B. Bohnet, and R. McDonald, “On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization,” arXiv (2 May 2020): [https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00661](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00661). \[8\] Anthropic, “Introducing the Model Context Protocol” (25 November 2024): [https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol).

# Part 3: Building an AI-powered assistant for investment research with multi-agent collaboration in Amazon Bedrock and Amazon Bedrock Data Automation

In the financial services industry, analysts need to switch between structured data (such as time-series pricing information), unstructured text (such as SEC filings and analyst reports), and audio/visual content (earnings calls and presentations). Each format requires different analytical approaches and specialized tools, creating workflow inefficiencies. Add on top of this the intense time pressure resulting from rapidly evolving industry conditions and narrow decision windows. Delayed analysis can mean missed opportunities or failure to identify emerging risks, with potentially significant financial consequences.

AI-assistants boost productivity by automating routine data collection and processing tasks, surfacing relevant insights, and allowing analysts to focus on higher-value activities. However, a single AI agent struggles with complex, multistep investment research workflows to effectively handle the full spectrum of multiple specialized tasks. This is where multi-agent collaboration provides an advancement. By creating specialized AI subagents that excel in specific tasks and functionalities and using them together under a coordinated framework using an AI supervisor agent, we can address the full complexity of investment research workflows. A supervisor agent can intelligently decompose complex queries, delegate specialized tasks to subagents, and synthesize their outputs into comprehensive answers—similar to how a research team functions in the real world. The benefits of this approach are substantial, stemming from distributed problem-solving and specialization, improved accuracy through specialized expertise, enhanced scalability by adding new agent capabilities without rebuilding the entire system, and greater transparency because the reasoning process of each specialized agent can be tracked and verified.

[Amazon Bedrock Agents](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/agents/) uses the reasoning of [foundation models](https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/foundation-models/) (FMs), APIs, and data to break down user requests, gather relevant information, and efficiently complete tasks. With the [multi-agent collaboration](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/amazon-bedrock-announces-general-availability-of-multi-agent-collaboration/) capability of [Amazon Bedrock](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/), you can build, deploy, and manage multiple AI agents working together on complex multistep tasks that require specialized skills. [Amazon Bedrock Data Automation](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/bda/) (BDA) enables the generation of useful insights from unstructured multimodal content such as documents, images, audio, and video for your AI-powered applications, and it can be used as a parser when setting up a knowledge base for [Retrieval Augmented Generation](https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/retrieval-augmented-generation/) (RAG) workflows.

In this post, we walk through how to build a multi-agent investment research assistant using the multi-agent collaboration capability of Amazon Bedrock. Our solution demonstrates how a team of specialized AI agents can work together to analyze financial news, evaluate stock performance, optimize portfolio allocations, and deliver comprehensive investment insights—all orchestrated through a unified, natural language interface. We use BDA as a parser when setting up a knowledge base for RAG workflows with multi-modal data. Anthropic Claude models in Amazon Bedrock are used as the [large language models](https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/large-language-model/) (LLMs) associated with the supervisor agents and subagents.

Check the video that demonstrates the solution,

Results from these agents are demonstrative, not to be taken as financial advice.

## Solution overview

The multi-agent investment research assistant is composed of a supervisor agent and three subagents (quantitative analysis agent, news agent, and smart summarizer agent) working together in a coordinated framework, as shown in the following diagram.

![](https://d2908q01vomqb2.cloudfront.net/f1f836cb4ea6efb2a0b1b99f41ad8b103eff4b59/2025/05/19/multi-agent-1.jpg)

The supervisor agent uses the instructions you provide to understand the structure and role of each collaborator agent. The supervisor agent orchestrates the overall investment research process by breaking down user prompts, delegating subtasks to specialized subagents, and consolidating their outputs to generate the final response. Each subagent is configured to carry out specialized tasks as follows:

- **Quantitative analysis agent**
  - **Functions** – Powered by stock-query and portfolio-optimization action groups.
  - **Responsibilities** – Query and analyze historical stock data and build optimized portfolio allocations based on user inputs such as stock tickers or investment amount.
- **News agent**
  - **Functions** – Powered by the `web_search` action group and the `financial_analysis_KB` knowledge base.
  - **Responsibilities** – Search and retrieve relevant financial data such as earnings reports and filings from the knowledge base for context. If information isn’t present in the knowledge base, construct a web query. Handles queries related to analyzing company financials, performance drivers, and management commentary.
- **Smart summarizer agent**
  - **Functions** – Uses LLM capabilities with specific prompt instructions, no custom action group.
  - **Responsibilities** – Takes in output from other subagents, such as recent news and financial data, and synthesizes the information into structured investment insights. Summarizes long texts or documents such as earnings reports into concise insights.

Multi-agent collaboration works as follows:

1. User prompts the multi-agent investment research assistant with a high-level research query.
2. Supervisor agent breaks the query into subtasks and orchestrates and invokes the relevant subagents in parallel or sequentially as needed:
   - News agent to retrieve financial reports or data
   - Quantitative analysis agent for stock data analysis
   - Smart summarizer agent to summarize lengthy texts
3. Supervisor agent consolidates the subagent outputs along with the original query context.
4. Supervisor agent passes the consolidated information to the LLM to generate the final research insights.

This architecture uses the strengths of the different specialized agents to comprehensively address the various aspects of investment research, and the supervisor agent provides the intelligent orchestration.

## Technical architecture

The following diagram is the technical architecture.

![](https://d2908q01vomqb2.cloudfront.net/f1f836cb4ea6efb2a0b1b99f41ad8b103eff4b59/2025/05/15/technical.jpg)

Some of the key components and the associated codes of the technical architecture are:

- **Amazon Bedrock Data Automation (BDA) for processing data** – The following invokes a BDA job asynchronously using the `invoke_data_automation_async()` method from the `bda-runtime` client in `boto3`:





```python
bda_response = bda_runtime_client.invoke_data_automation_async(
              inputConfiguration={
                  "s3Uri": s3_uri_input
              },
              outputConfiguration={
                  "s3Uri": s3_uri_output
              },
          )
```





Python

- **Defining and building subagents with tools** – The following creates a subagent using `Agent.create.` It defines two Lambda tools—one for fetching historical stock data and another for running portfolio optimization—then registers them with the subagent along with its role, goal, instructions, and the underlying FM.





```python
quantitative_analysis_agent = Agent.create(
      name="quantitative_analysis_agent",
      role="Financial Data Collector",
      goal="Retrieve real-time and historic stock prices as well as optimizing a portfolio given tickers.",
      instructions="""Specialist in real-time financial data extraction and portfolio optimization.
                          ...""",
      tools=[\
          # Stock Data Lookup Tool\
          {\
              "code": stock_data_lookup_arn,\
              "definition": {\
                  "name": "stock_data_lookup",\
                  "description": "Gets the 1-month stock price history for a given stock ticker, formatted as JSON.",\
                  "parameters": {\
                      "ticker": {"description": "The ticker to retrieve price history for", "type": "string", "required": True}\
                  },\
              },\
          },\
      ],
      llm=LLM,
)
```





Python


- **Defining and building the supervisor agent (multi-agent investment research assistant)** – The following creates a supervisor agent using `SupervisorAgent.create`. It defines the collaborator agents (subagents), and registers them with the supervisor agent along with its role, goal, instructions, and the underlying FM.





```python
investment_research_assistant = SupervisorAgent.create(
      "investment_research_assistant",
      role="Investment Research Assistant",
      goal="A seasoned investment research expert responsible for orchestrating subagents ...",
      collaboration_type="SUPERVISOR",
      instructions=f"""You are a Investment Research Assistant, a financial research supervisor overseeing multiple specialized agents. Your goal is to coordinate and synthesize their outputs to create a structured stock investment analysis.
                  ...""",
      collaborator_agents=[\
          {\
              "agent": "news_agent",\
              "instructions": f"Always check the knowledge base (ID: {kb_id}) first. Use this collaborator for finding news and analyzing specific documents."\
          },\
          {\
              "agent": "quantitative_analysis_agent",\
              "instructions": "Use this collaborator for retrieving stock price history and performing portfolio optimization."\
          },\
          {\
              "agent": "smart_summarizer_agent",\
              "instructions": "Use this collaborator for synthesizing stock trends, financial data, and generating structured investment insights."\
          }\
      ],
      collaborator_objects=[news_agent, quantitative_analysis_agent, smart_summarizer_agent],
      llm=LLM,
)
```





Python

- **Invoking the supervisor agent (multi-agent investment research assistant)** – The following uses the `invoke` method to pass in the defined request as a prompt and get response from the supervisor agent, using the multi-agent collaboration architecture.





```python
request = """What's AMZN stock price doing over the last week
                 and relate that to recent news"""
print(f"Request:\n{request}\n")
result = investment_research_assistant.invoke(
      request,
      enable_trace=True,
      trace_level="core",
)
print(f"Final answer:\n{result}")
```





Python


## Prerequisites

To deploy this solution, attach the appropriate permissions for Amazon Bedrock, [AWS Lambda](https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/), [Amazon OpenSearch Serverless](https://aws.amazon.com/opensearch-service/features/serverless/), [Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) (Amazon S3), and [AWS Identity and Access Management](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/) (IAM) to your execution role. You also need to deploy two [AWS CloudFormation](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudformation/) stacks: `web_search` and `stock_data`.

For more information on the permission policy, CloudFormation stacks, and setting up the environment, refer to the [README.md in the investment\_research\_agent](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-bedrock-agent-samples/blob/main/examples/multi_agent_collaboration/investment_research_agent/README.md#prerequisites) repository on GitHub.

## Dive deeper into the solution

To dive deeper into the solution and the code shown in this post, refer to the [investment research assistant agent GitHub repo](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-bedrock-agent-samples/blob/main/examples/multi_agent_collaboration/investment_research_agent/README.md). The repo contains instructions for the end-to-end solution, including setting up the supervisor agent and subagents, associated action groups, unstructured data (earnings reports PDF files and earnings call audio files), and structured data (stocks time series).

In the appendix at the end of this post, we share different questions asked by a financial analyst to the supervisor agent, the subagent invoked, and the answers from the supervisor agent.

## Cleanup

To clean up this solution, make sure to delete all agents, knowledge bases, and associated Amazon OpenSearch Serverless instances. Delete both the `web_search` and `stock_data` stacks. This will make sure that no charges are incurred after you’re finished testing this solution. Read the [associated notebook](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-bedrock-agent-samples/blob/main/examples/multi_agent_collaboration/investment_research_agent/main.ipynb) in the GitHub repository for more instructions on cleaning up the agents.

## Conclusion

The multi-agent investment research assistant built using Amazon Bedrock Agents with its multi-agent collaboration feature represents how financial professionals can use AI to enhance their analytical capabilities. By using specialized agents—each focusing on distinct aspects of financial analysis—orchestrated by a supervisor agent, we’ve created a system that can process diverse data types, apply appropriate analytical techniques, and synthesize comprehensive insights in response to complex investment queries. The result is an AI assistant that can handle multistep financial analysis workflows that would otherwise overwhelm a single-agent system.

The application of multi-agent collaboration extends broadly across the financial sector, from equity research and portfolio management to risk assessment and compliance monitoring. A complex financial workflow that requires multiple specialized skills and data sources can benefit from this architectural approach.

The implementation uses key AWS services, including Amazon Bedrock for foundation models, the multi-agent collaboration capability of Amazon Bedrock Agents, Amazon S3 for document storage, Amazon OpenSearch Serverless for vector search capabilities, and Lambda functions for specialized actions. This serverless architecture provides scalability, and the knowledge base integration provides context from financial documents to ground the AI’s responses in factual data.

For more information:

- Get started **building your own** multi-agent investment research assistant by accessing our complete solution code and implementation guide in the [GitHub repo.](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-bedrock-agent-samples/blob/main/examples/multi_agent_collaboration/investment_research_agent/README.md)
- Explore how to **customize the solution** for your specific investment research needs by modifying the subagents’ specialized tools and action groups and by bringing your data securely to [Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/knowledge-bases/).
- You can also explore and run [Amazon Bedrock multi-agent collaboration workshop](https://catalog.us-east-1.prod.workshops.aws/workshops/1031afa5-be84-4a6a-9886-4e19ce67b9c2/en-US) with AWS specialists or on your own.

Read more about single agent solutions for investment research with multi-modal data:

- **Part 1**– [Generative AI and multi-modal agents in AWS: The key to unlocking new value in financial markets](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/generative-ai-and-multi-modal-agents-in-aws-the-key-to-unlocking-new-value-in-financial-markets/), which demonstrates how multi-modal agents use AWS services to efficiently gather financial insights from an organization’s text, speech, and tabular data sources for a financial analyst’s query using open source LangChain agents as alternative implementation.
- **Part 2**– [AI-powered assistants for investment research with multi-modal data: An application of Amazon Bedrock Agents](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/ai-powered-assistants-for-investment-research-with-multi-modal-data-an-application-of-amazon-bedrock-agents/), which introduces a solution using Amazon Bedrock Agents and Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases that can help financial analysts use various multi-modal financial data to gather financial insights.

Read the blog post on [Unlocking complex problem-solving with multi-agent collaboration on Amazon Bedrock](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/unlocking-complex-problem-solving-with-multi-agent-collaboration-on-amazon-bedrock/) for more details on benefits, design, and evaluation of multi-agent collaboration for enterprise applications.
